key_matchups: Edson Álvarez winning the ball before Romo’s shot chance
Denis Višinský creating and taking two Czechia attempts on goal
phases.summary: The opening phase is lively and open, with both sides generating early chances rather than settling into a cautious block. Czechia starts brightly and looks especially dangerous on the break or from loose balls, while Mexico’s best moment comes from Edson Álvarez regaining possession and sparking a shot opportunity for Romo.

adjustments: The game’s tempo slows noticeably after the hydration break, with both sides struggling to create chances
Czechia show slightly more intent around the box through Denis Višinský and set-piece pressure, but no clear shape change is described
key_matchups: Denis Višinský vs Mexico's defensive coverage in the box
Israel Reyes vs Czechia's clearance line on the acrobatic attempt
Gilberto Mora and Roberto Alvarado combining against Czechia's midfield/defensive pressure
Julián Quiñones vs Czechia's challenge in a dangerous area
phases.summary: After an even opening, this phase becomes more stop-start and subdued, with injury delays and a hydration break contributing to a noticeable drop in rhythm. Czechia begin to edge into the more dangerous moments through Denis Višinský’s individual quality and a late free-kick, but Mexico also create flashes through open-play combinations, leaving the contest balanced and largely blunt before halftime.

formations_blocks: both sides in a cautious/block-oriented setup
phases.summary: The first half was described as unusually cagey and lacking big moments, with a combined xG of just 0.46. No tactical tweaks or substitutions were made at the break, so both teams resumed unchanged for the second half.

formations_blocks: Mexico building from the back and probing through midfield
Czechia defending their back line and later pushing forward in search of a goal
Czechia vulnerable to breakaways and attacks down the left-hand side
adjustments: Mexico shifted from long balls from the back to more success when recycling possession and playing through midfield
Czechia increased their attacking intent after conceding, but their final service remained inaccurate
Mexico exploited space with a marauding forward run from Jorge Sánchez and a breakaway finish from Mateo Chávez
key_matchups: Jorge Sánchez vs the Czech left side/defensive line
Mateo Chávez vs the Czech defense on the break
Julián Quiñones vs Czechia's recovering defense
phases.summary: The phase turned on Mexico’s ability to move from direct long balls to more effective recycled attacks through midfield, which led to their opener and then a second goal moments later. Czechia were left exposed, particularly down their left side, and although they reacted by pushing forward after 58 minutes, their delivery into the box was consistently poor.

formations_blocks: Czechia pushing forward in search of a late chance
scrappy open-play/transition phase in stoppage time
adjustments: Guillermo Ochoa comes on for Rangel at 77'
Tomáš Souček is forced off injured and replaced by Alexandr Sojka at 85'
late-game play becomes loose and experimental as players attempt audacious actions
key_matchups: Guillermo Ochoa vs Czechia’s late closing/surge attempts
Tomáš Souček closing down Guillermo Ochoa before his injury
phases.summary: Mexico saw out the final quarter with sustained possession and complete crowd control, while Czechia tried repeatedly to surge forward but rarely built anything of substance. The phase was also defined by the emotional arrival of Guillermo Ochoa and a stoppage-time flourish, with Mexico adding a third goal from a broken play after Ochoa’s long ball helped set it up.
